sources.notes: Read the full statement and aggregate bounds: sum n, sum m1, and sum m2 are each at most 200000. The target is equality of the two connectivity relations, not equality of edge sets.
The official editorial confirms the two-stage optimum: remove every F edge crossing G components, then add the difference between the remaining F component count and the G component count.
Cross-checked a clean two-DSU implementation that counts G components, unions only admissible F edges, and outputs forced removals plus cf-cg.
Independently proved both lower bounds and achievability, then exhaustively compared the formula with all target graphs and starting graphs on n up to 4 (4165 graph pairs).

annotation.summary: Given undirected graphs F and G on the same vertices, toggle the minimum number of edges of F so that F and G induce exactly the same connected-component partition.
annotation.constraints: t <= 10000; per test n,m1,m2 <= 200000; the sums of n, m1, and m2 over all tests are each <= 200000. F edges must be retained until G's components are known.
annotation.core_idea: First compute the component id compG[v] in G. Every F edge whose endpoints have different compG values is forced to be deleted: retaining even one would connect two vertices that G separates. Union all other F edges in a second DSU, producing F' whose components lie wholly inside G components. If a G component contains r components of F', at least r-1 added edges are necessary and sufficient to connect them. Summed over G components, additions equal cc(F')-cc(G). Therefore the exact answer is cross_edges(F,G) + cc(F') - cc(G).
annotation.complexity: O((n + m1 + m2) alpha(n)) time and O(n + m1) extra memory per test.
tricks: Treat the requested connectivity relation as a partition of the vertex set.
Delete all edges crossing target components before counting missing connections.
Within each target component, r surviving components need exactly r-1 added edges.

solution_variants.name: forced-cuts-component-deficit-dsu
solution_variants.summary: Build a DSU for G, count and discard F edges crossing its components, union every remaining F edge in a second DSU, and add cc(F')-cc(G). DFS component labels can replace either DSU but do not materially change the argument.
solution_variants.complexity: O((n + m1 + m2) alpha(n)) time and O(n + m1) extra memory.

tags.evidence: The required final property is equality of connected-component partitions, and the optimum is expressed entirely through target and surviving component counts.
Two DSUs provide the intended linear-time implementation: one identifies G components and one counts components formed by admissible F edges.
The proof decomposes the task by G component; each contains r surviving F components and contributes independently exactly r-1 additions.
